title: Kaffa Espresso Bar — Live Client Site
tagline: A neighborhood espresso bar's first real web presence — vanilla site, real deploy pipeline, HTTPS that renews itself.
summary: A Jersey City espresso bar with no web presence and no in-house technical capacity needed a real site, a real domain, and an operator who could hand it over and have it stay up. The result is a single-page vanilla HTML/CSS/JS site on its own droplet with a release-versioned deploy pipeline, atomic symlink cutover, 5-release rollback, and zero-downtime cert renewal.
role_scope: Designer, engineer, operator. One-month build, single client, hand-off-ready.
stack: Bash deploy scripts with post-receive hooks
resume_bullets: Built a single-page vanilla HTML/CSS/JS site (no framework tax) with 18 WebP images at ~2.3 MB total, mobile hamburger nav, smooth scroll, and SEO meta + structured data.
Wrote a release-versioned deploy pipeline (bootstrap, deploy, remote worker, Nginx vhost) with atomic symlink cutover and a 5-release rollback window.

Migrated the live site from a legacy subdomain to a real apex `.com` without touching MX / TXT / nameservers — no email outage, no DNS-cutover downtime, legacy cert kept working through the transition.
demonstrates: Picking boring infrastructure over flashy: vanilla JS + Nginx + webroot ACME over a static-host SaaS, because the operator needs Nginx-level redirect control and zero-downtime renewal.
Deploy primitives that survive a client hand-off — bootstrap, deploy, rollback, vhost config, all in version control.
Domain-cutover work with surgical care for the non-web stuff (email, third-party integrations on legacy DNS).
proof: ~2.3 MB total page weight including all 18 hero / menu / gallery photos.
outcomes: Live at the custom domain, primary online presence for the shop — production-stable since Mar 9.
constraints: No in-house technical capacity at the shop — every deploy primitive had to be operable by the client or trivially handed off.
Legacy subdomain hosted off another artist's portfolio, with email and external integrations still pointed at the old DNS — cutover had to be surgical.

case_study_sections.title: What I built
case_study_sections.summary: A vanilla HTML5 / CSS3 / JS single-page site — hero, about, menu, gallery, hours, location, contact, mobile hamburger nav, smooth scroll. 18 WebP images optimized to ~2.3 MB total. Teal accent (#3FBFBF), Pacifico script logo. A release-versioned deploy pipeline: bootstrap script, deploy script, remote worker script, Nginx vhost config — atomic symlink cutover with a 5-release rollback window and a deploy-only SSH user. Let's Encrypt HTTPS via Certbot's webroot ACME flow so renewal doesn't take the site down. SEO pass on the final commit (meta, OG tags, structured data).

case_study_sections.title: One sharp decision
case_study_sections.summary: Pulling the site off AWS S3 and rebuilding as a git-driven droplet deploy. S3 is the easy answer for a static site — but I wanted Nginx-level redirect control, real release versioning, and the same deploy primitives I use across other client work. Webroot ACME over standalone for the same reason: HTTPS renews without a downtime window. Same instinct as the duSraBheja pivot: pick the slightly harder option you can actually own.

case_study_sections.title: One real struggle
case_study_sections.summary: Domain migration was the hairy part. Legacy domain was a subdomain of another artist's portfolio; the client wanted a real `.com` apex while keeping email and external integrations pointed at the old DNS untouched. I explicitly walled off MX / TXT / nameserver records, wrote a `finalize_primary_domain_cutover.sh` that set up `www` → apex and old-subdomain → apex redirects, and kept the legacy cert working through the cutover. No outage, no broken email.

case_study_sections.title: Outcome
case_study_sections.summary: Live at the custom domain, primary online presence for the shop. Production-stable since Mar 9, 2026.
